steps.title: State the final solutions
steps.content:
From the GDC, the $x$-coordinates (time $t$) of the intersections are approximately:

$$t \approx 1.45,\ 6.95,\ 13.4,\ 19.0$$

These values are rounded to 3 significant figures as required. These represent the times (in hours after midnight) when the water height is exactly $2.0$ metres.

parts.content: Find the first time \(t>0\) when the gauges read the same height, i.e. \(f(t)=g(t)\). Give \(t\) to 3 s.f. (hours).
parts.markscheme:
- Set up equation \(f(t)=g(t)\) <marks>M1</marks>
- Attempt to solve using calculator or graph <marks>M1</marks>
- First solution: \(t \approx 2.12\) h <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
To find when the gauges read the same height, we need to find the value of $t$ where $f(t) = g(t)$. This means we are looking for the intersection points of the two functions:

$$1.7 + 2.3\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t - 1.2)\right) = 1.2 + 1.8\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t - 3.1)\right)$$

This is the first step toward earning your marks.

steps.title: Enter functions into GDC
steps.content: Since this is a complex trigonometric equation, the most efficient IB-approved method is to use your Graphing Display Calculator (GDC). We will plot both models as separate functions and look for where they cross.

gdc.instructions: In the Graph menu, define $f_1(x) = 1.7 + 2.3\sin((\pi/6)(x - 1.2))$ and $f_2(x) = 1.2 + 1.8\cos((\pi/6)(x - 3.1))$. Ensure the angle unit is set to RADIANS.

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=]. Enter $Y_1 = 1.7 + 2.3\sin((\pi/6)(X - 1.2))$ and $Y_2 = 1.2 + 1.8\cos((\pi/6)(X - 3.1))$. Ensure your calculator is in RADIANS mode.

desmos.instructions: Graph the two functions $y = 1.7 + 2.3\sin((\pi/6)(x - 1.2))$ and $y = 1.2 + 1.8\cos((\pi/6)(x - 3.1))$.

calculator.description: Enter the height models into your graphing software or calculator.

steps.title: Adjust the viewing window
steps.content: Set your window to match the domain given in the question: $0 \le t < 24$. For the $y$-axis (height), a range of $-1$ to $5$ should be sufficient to see the full oscillations.

steps.title: Find the first intersection
steps.content:
Use the intersection tool on your calculator to find the first point where the curves meet for $t > 0$. 

Looking at the graph, the curves cross several times, but we specifically need the very first instance after midnight ($t=0$).

gdc.instructions: Use the 'Analyze Graph' or 'G-Solv' tool and select 'Intersection'. Move the selection boundaries to include only the first positive intersection point.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select '5: intersect'. Move the cursor near the first crossing point and press [ENTER] three times.

desmos.instructions: Click on the first point where the two graphs intersect for $x > 0$.

calculator.description: Identify the first intersection point for positive x.

steps.title: Final answer (3 s.f.)
steps.content:
The intersection tool gives an $x$-coordinate (which is our time $t$) of approximately $2.1157...$ 

Rounding to 3 significant figures as requested, we get:

$$t = 2.12 \text{ hours}$$

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
To find when the gauges read the same height, we need to set the two functions equal to each other:

$$1.7 + 2.3 \sin \left( \frac{\pi}{6}(t - 1.2) \right) = 1.2 + 1.8 \cos \left( \frac{\pi}{6}(t - 3.1) \right)$$

This is what the markscheme refers to as setting up the equation $f(t) = g(t)$. Since we are looking for the first time $t > 0$, we will look for the smallest positive value of $t$ that satisfies this.

steps.title: Configure the calculator
steps.content:
Before solving, ensure your graphing calculator (GDC) is set to **Radians** mode. This is crucial because the argument of the trigonometric functions includes $\pi$. 

You can use the numerical solver tool (often called `nSolve` or `Solver`) to find the value of $t$. To find the *first* solution after $t = 0$, it is helpful to provide a guess or a starting value close to $0$ (like $t = 1$).

gdc.instructions: Use the 'Equation Solver' tool. Enter the equation f(t) - g(t) = 0. Set a lower bound of 0 and an initial guess of 1. Solve for the variable t.

ti84.instructions: Go to [MATH], select [B:Solver...]. Enter the equation as E1: 1.7 + 2.3*sin(π/6*(X - 1.2)) and E2: 1.2 + 1.8*cos(π/6*(X - 3.1)). Set X=1 as a guess and press [ALPHA][SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Graph y = 1.7 + 2.3sin(π/6(x - 1.2)) and y = 1.2 + 1.8cos(π/6(x - 3.1)). Find the first point of intersection where x > 0.

calculator.description: Using a numerical solver to find the first positive intersection.

steps.title: Solve and round
steps.content:
The numerical solver provides the result:

$$t \approx 2.1243...$$

The question asks for the answer to 3 significant figures. Rounding $2.1243...$ to three digits gives:

$$t = 2.12 \text{ hours}$$

This matches the requirement for the final accuracy mark in the markscheme.

steps.title: Equate the gauge heights
steps.content:
To find when the heights of the two gauges are identical, we need to find the value of $t$ for which the functions are equal:

$$f(t) = g(t)$$

Substituting the given models:

$$1.7 + 2.3\sin\!\Big(\tfrac{\pi}{6}(t-1.2)\Big) = 1.2 + 1.8\cos\!\Big(\tfrac{\pi}{6}(t-3.1)\Big)$$ 

This marks the first step in solving the problem as per the markscheme.

steps.title: Define the root function
steps.content:
Instead of looking for where two graphs intersect, we can define a single function $h(t)$ by subtracting one from the other:

$$h(t) = f(t) - g(t)$$

Finding the time when the gauges read the same height is now equivalent to finding the **roots** (the x-intercepts) of $h(t)$:

$$h(t) = 1.7 + 2.3\sin\!\Big(\tfrac{\pi}{6}(t-1.2)\Big) - \left(1.2 + 1.8\cos\!\Big(\tfrac{\pi}{6}(t-3.1)\Big)\right) = 0$$

steps.title: Use a graphing calculator
steps.content:
Now, use your graphing calculator to find the first positive root. This approach is recommended in **Topic 2.10: Solving equations graphically**.

**Important:** Ensure your calculator is set to **Radian mode**, as the trigonometric arguments contain $\pi$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graph menu.
2. Enter the function h(x) = 1.7 + 2.3*sin((π/6)*(x - 1.2)) - (1.2 + 1.8*cos((π/6)*(x - 3.1))).
3. Draw the graph and use the G-Solv (Graph Solver) tool.
4. Select 'Root' to find the first x-intercept where x > 0.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=] and enter Y1 = 1.7 + 2.3*sin((π/6)*(X - 1.2)) - (1.2 + 1.8*cos((π/6)*(X - 3.1))).
2. Press [GRAPH]. (Adjust window to X:[0, 24] if needed).
3. Press [2nd] [TRACE] and select '2: zero'.
4. Follow the prompts for 'Left Bound', 'Right Bound', and 'Guess' near the first intercept.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type the expression h(x) = 1.7 + 2.3*sin((π/6)*(x - 1.2)) - (1.2 + 1.8*cos((π/6)*(x - 3.1))) into the entry bar.
2. Look at the graph for the first point where the curve crosses the x-axis for x > 0.
3. Click on that intercept point to see its coordinates.

calculator.description: Graph the function h(t) and find the first positive x-intercept.

steps.title: Identify the first solution
steps.content:
By using the zero-finding tool, we find that the first positive root occurs at:

$$t \approx 2.12429...$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures as requested, we get:

$$t = 2.12\text{ hours}$$

parts.content: Over \(0\le t<24\), determine the intervals where \(f(t)\ge g(t)\). Give endpoints to 3 s.f.
parts.markscheme:
- Find all intersection points in domain: \(t \approx 2.12,\ 9.68,\ 14.1,\ 21.7\) <marks>M1</marks>
- Test subintervals to determine inequality <marks>M1</marks>
- State correct intervals: \([2.12,\ 9.68] \cup [14.1,\ 21.7]\) <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define the functions
steps.content:
To find the intervals where $f(t) \ge g(t)$, we first need to input both models into our graphing calculator. This approach allows us to visually compare the heights of the two tide gauges over the 24-hour period.

$$f(t)=1.7+2.3\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t-1.2)\right)$$
$$g(t)=1.2+1.8\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t-3.1)\right)$$ 

We set our graphing window for $0 \le t < 24$ to match the domain given in the question.

gdc.instructions: Go to Graph mode. Enter both equations. Set the X-window from 0 to 24. Press [DRAW].

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=]. Enter $f(t)$ as Y1 and $g(t)$ as Y2. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin = 0, Xmax = 24. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions: Type both equations into separate rows. Adjust the x-axis scale from 0 to 24 using the wrench icon.

calculator.description: Enter functions and set the domain window.

steps.title: Find intersection points
steps.content:
Next, use the calculator's intersection tool to find every value of $t$ where the two graphs cross ($f(t) = g(t)$). These points define the boundaries of the intervals we are looking for.

Within the domain $[0, 24)$, there are four intersection points:

$$t \approx 2.12, \quad t \approx 9.68, \quad t \approx 14.1, \quad t \approx 21.7$$

gdc.instructions: Press [F5] (G-Solv) and then [F5] (ISCT). Use the arrow keys to toggle between the different intersection points.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [TRACE] [5: intersect]. Move the cursor near a crossing point and press [ENTER] three times. Repeat for all four points.

desmos.instructions: Click on the points where the two graphs intersect. Desmos will display the coordinates $(t, y)$. Record the $t$-values.

calculator.description: Find where the two curves intersect.

steps.title: Identify intervals of dominance
steps.content:
The question asks for where $f(t) \ge g(t)$. Visually inspecting the graph, we look for the segments where the curve for $f(t)$ is physically "above" or touching the curve for $g(t)$.

- Between $t = 0$ and $2.12$, $g(t)$ is higher.
- Between $t = 2.12$ and $9.68$, $f(t)$ is higher.
- Between $t = 9.68$ and $14.1$, $g(t)$ is higher.
- Between $t = 14.1$ and $21.7$, $f(t)$ is higher.
- Between $t = 21.7$ and $24$, $g(t)$ is higher.

steps.title: State the final intervals
steps.content:
Combining the visual data with the intersection points rounded to 3 significant figures, the intervals where $f(t) \ge g(t)$ are:

$$[2.12, 9.68] \cup [14.1, 21.7]$$

Each mark is awarded for:
1. Identifying the four intersection points ($2.12, 9.68, 14.1, 21.7$).
2. Correctly testing or visually identifying the subintervals where the inequality holds.
3. Stating the final intervals using correct notation and significant figures.

steps.title: Define the Difference Function
steps.content:
To solve the inequality $f(t) \ge g(t)$, we can rearrange it to $f(t) - g(t) \ge 0$. Let's define a new function $h(t)$ representing the difference between the two gauges:

$$h(t) = f(t) - g(t)$$

$$h(t) = 1.7 + 2.3\sin\!\Big(\tfrac{\pi}{6}(t-1.2)\Big) - \left(1.2 + 1.8\cos\!\Big(\tfrac{\pi}{6}(t-3.1)\Big)\right)$$

By finding the values where $h(t) \ge 0$, we are finding exactly when the first gauge is higher than or equal to the second.

steps.title: Find the Zeros
steps.content:
The intersection points occur when $h(t) = 0$. Using a graphing calculator, we find the zeros of $h(t)$ within the domain $0 \le t < 24$. These zeros mark the boundaries of our intervals.

From the GDC, the zeros are approximately:
$$t \approx 2.12,\ 9.68,\ 14.1,\ 21.7$$

These points are where the water levels are equal.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graph mode.
2. Input the function h(x) = f(x) - g(x).
3. Set the view window X from 0 to 24.
4. Use the G-Solv (F5) then Root (F1) function to find the zeros.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = 1.7 + 2.3*sin(π/6*(X-1.2)) - (1.2 + 1.8*cos(π/6*(X-3.1))).
3. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=24.
4. Press [GRAPH].
5. Press [2nd] [TRACE] and select '2: zero'.
6. Repeat for all four roots.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type h(x) = (1.7 + 2.3sin(π/6(x - 1.2))) - (1.2 + 1.8cos(π/6(x - 3.1))).
2. Look at the x-axis for the points where the curve crosses zero between x=0 and x=24.
3. Click the gray dots on the x-axis to reveal the coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the zeros of the difference function $h(t)$.

steps.title: Analyze the Graph
steps.content:
To satisfy $f(t) \ge g(t)$, we look for the intervals where the graph of $h(t)$ is on or above the horizontal $t$-axis ($h(t) \ge 0$). 

By inspecting the graph or testing values in each sub-interval:
- For $[0, 2.12)$, the graph is below the axis ($h(t) < 0$).
- For $[2.12, 9.68]$, the graph is above or on the axis ($h(t) \ge 0$).
- For $(9.68, 14.1)$, the graph is below the axis ($h(t) < 0$).
- For $[14.1, 21.7]$, the graph is above or on the axis ($h(t) \ge 0$).
- For $(21.7, 24)$, the graph is below the axis ($h(t) < 0$).

steps.title: State the Intervals
steps.content:
Based on our analysis, the first gauge is higher than or equal to the second gauge during the following time intervals:

$$[2.12,\ 9.68] \cup [14.1,\ 21.7]$$

This means $f(t) \ge g(t)$ roughly between 2:07 AM to 9:41 AM, and 2:06 PM to 9:42 PM.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
To find the intervals where $f(t) \ge g(t)$, we first need to find the specific times $t$ where the two gauges show the same height ($f(t) = g(t)$). These "boundary points" will divide our domain $0 \le t < 24$ into smaller sub-intervals.

We need to solve:
$$1.7 + 2.3\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t-1.2)\right) = 1.2 + 1.8\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t-3.1)\right)$$ 
over the domain $0 \le t < 24$.

steps.title: Find intersection points
steps.content:
Using the numerical solver on your GDC, find all values of $t$ in the range $[0, 24)$. These values are where the graphs of $f(t)$ and $g(t)$ cross each other.

Rounding to 3 significant figures, the intersection points are:
$$t \approx 2.12, \quad t \approx 9.68, \quad t \approx 14.1, \quad t \approx 21.7$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Equation/Solver menu. Enter the equation f(x) = g(x) and set the Lower bound to 0 and Upper bound to 24. Solve to find the four values.

ti84.instructions: Press MATH, then select B:Solver... Enter 0 = 1.7 + 2.3*sin(π/6*(X-1.2)) - (1.2 + 1.8*cos(π/6*(X-3.1))). Provide initial guesses like 2, 10, 14, and 21 to find all solutions.

desmos.instructions: Type the equations for f(x) and g(x). Look for the intersection points between x=0 and x=24.

calculator.description: Find the roots of the equation f(t) - g(t) = 0 using the Solver tool.

steps.title: Identify test intervals
steps.content:
The intersection points create five distinct sub-intervals within our domain $[0, 24)$:
1. $[0, 2.12)$
2. $(2.12, 9.68)$
3. $(9.68, 14.1)$
4. $(14.1, 21.7)$
5. $(21.7, 24)$

In each interval, either $f(t) \ge g(t)$ is true for the whole interval, or it is false for the whole interval. We just need to test one point from each.

steps.title: Test points in intervals
steps.content:
Pick a value of $t$ in each interval and compare $f(t)$ and $g(t)$:

- **Interval 2 ($t=5$):** 
  $f(5) \approx 3.80$ and $g(5) \approx 2.18$
  Since $3.80 > 2.18$, the inequality **holds**.

- **Interval 3 ($t=12$):** 
  $f(12) \approx 0.35$ and $g(12) \approx 1.11$
  Since $0.35 < 1.11$, the inequality **fails**.

- **Interval 4 ($t=18$):** 
  $f(18) \approx 3.05$ and $g(18) \approx 1.29$
  Since $3.05 > 1.29$, the inequality **holds**.

By checking the remaining intervals ($t=1$ and $t=23$), you will find the inequality fails there too.

steps.title: State the final intervals
steps.content:
Based on our testing, $f(t) \ge g(t)$ in the second and fourth intervals. 

We include the endpoints because the inequality is "greater than or equal to" ($\ge$).

$$[2.12, 9.68] \cup [14.1, 21.7]$$

highlights.text: determine the intervals where f(t)\ge g(t)

parts.content:
Let \(h(t)=f(t)-g(t)\). Show that \(h\) can be written in the form
\[
h(t)=R\sin\!\Big(\tfrac{\pi}{6}t-\varphi\Big)+C,
\]
and determine \(R\), \(\varphi\) (in radians) and \(C\) correct to 3 s.f.
parts.markscheme:
- Expand \(f(t)\) and \(g(t)\) using addition formulae <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate coefficients of \(\sin(\tfrac{\pi}{6}t)\) and \(\cos(\tfrac{\pi}{6}t)\): \(A \approx 0.0632\), \(B \approx -1.26\) <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate \(R\), \(\varphi\), and \(C\): \(R=\sqrt{A^2+B^2} \approx 1.26\), \(\varphi=\arctan(-B/A) \approx 1.52\), \(C=0.500\) <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define the combined function
steps.content:
We are given $h(t) = f(t) - g(t)$. First, let's write out the full expression for $h(t)$ using the provided models for $f(t)$ and $g(t)$:

$$h(t) = \left[ 1.7 + 2.3 \sin \left( \frac{\pi}{6}t - 0.2\pi \right) \right] - \left[ 1.2 + 1.8 \cos \left( \frac{\pi}{6}t - \frac{3.1\pi}{6} \right) \right]$$

Notice that the constant term $C$ is simply the difference between the constant parts of $f(t)$ and $g(t)$:

$$C = 1.7 - 1.2 = 0.5$$

steps.title: Expand using compound angles
steps.content:
To combine the trigonometric parts, we use the compound angle identities from the data booklet:

$$\sin(A - B) = \sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B$$
$$\cos(A - B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$$

Applying these to the terms in $h(t)$ with $\theta = \frac{\pi}{6}t$:

$$2.3 \sin(\theta - 0.2\pi) = 2.3[\sin \theta \cos(0.2\pi) - \cos \theta \sin(0.2\pi)]$$
$$1.8 \cos(\theta - \tfrac{3.1\pi}{6}) = 1.8[\cos \theta \cos(\tfrac{3.1\pi}{6}) + \sin \theta \sin(\tfrac{3.1\pi}{6})]$$

steps.title: Collect sine and cosine
steps.content:
Now, we subtract the expanded cosine expression from the expanded sine expression and collect terms for $\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right)$ and $\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right)$. This gives:

$$h(t) = 0.5 + A \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right) + B \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right)$$

where the coefficients $A$ and $B$ are defined as:
$$A = 2.3 \cos(0.2\pi) - 1.8 \sin\left(\frac{3.1\pi}{6}\right)$$
$$B = -2.3 \sin(0.2\pi) - 1.8 \cos\left(\frac{3.1\pi}{6}\right)$$

Using a calculator in radians mode:
$$A \approx 0.0632$$
$$B \approx -1.26$$

gdc.instructions: Set calculator to Radians. Evaluate the coefficients.

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is in RADIANS. Enter the expressions for A and B.

desmos.instructions: Type the expressions directly to find values for A and B.

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficients A and B.

steps.title: Apply the harmonic identity
steps.content:
We want to write $A \sin \theta + B \cos \theta$ in the form $R \sin(\theta - \varphi)$. 
Expanding $R \sin(\theta - \varphi) = R \sin \theta \cos \varphi - R \cos \theta \sin \varphi$, we equate the coefficients:

$$\begin{aligned} R \cos \varphi &= A \\ R \sin \varphi &= -B \end{aligned}$$

From these, we find:

$$R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2} \approx \sqrt{0.0632^2 + (-1.26)^2} \approx 1.26$$

$$\tan \varphi = \frac{-B}{A} \approx \frac{1.26}{0.0632} \implies \varphi = \arctan\left(\frac{1.26}{0.0632}\right) \approx 1.52 \text{ rad}$$

gdc.instructions: Find R and phi using previous results.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the square root of A squared plus B squared, then the arctangent of -B/A.

desmos.instructions: Calculate R and phi.

calculator.description: Calculate R and phi.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
Combining everything, we have found the required parameters (rounded to 3 significant figures):

*   $R = 1.26$
*   $\varphi = 1.52$
*   $C = 0.500$

Thus, $h(t) = 1.26 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t - 1.52\right) + 0.500$.

steps.title: Define the difference function
steps.content:
First, we define the function $h(t) = f(t) - g(t)$ by subtracting the two given models:

$$h(t) = \left[1.7 + 2.3\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t - 1.2)\right)\right] - \left[1.2 + 1.8\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t - 3.1)\right)\right]$$

Notice that the constant part (the vertical shift) is simply the difference between the constants in $f(t)$ and $g(t)$:

$$C = 1.7 - 1.2 = 0.5$$

This gives us $h(t) = 2.3\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t - 1.2)\right) - 1.8\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(t - 3.1)\right) + 0.5$.

steps.title: Graph the function
steps.content: To find $R$ and $\phi$ using a GDC, we graph $h(t)$ for the domain $0 \le t < 24$. We input the expression for $h(t)$ directly into the graphing menu.

gdc.instructions:
In the graph menu, enter h(x) = 1.7 + 2.3sin((π/6)(x - 1.2)) - (1.2 + 1.8cos((π/6)(x - 3.1))).
Set the domain to 0 ≤ x ≤ 24.
View the graph.

ti84.instructions:
Go to [Y=].
Enter Y1 = 1.7 + 2.3 * sin((π/6) * (X - 1.2))
Enter Y2 = 1.2 + 1.8 * cos((π/6) * (X - 3.1))
Enter Y3 = Y1 - Y2
Go to [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=24, Ymin=-2, Ymax=3.
Press [GRAPH].

calculator.description: Graph the function h(t) and set the viewing window.

steps.title: Find amplitude R
steps.content:
The amplitude $R$ is half the distance between the maximum and minimum $y$-values of the graph. Using the GDC's calculation tools, we find:

*   $y_{\text{max}} \approx 1.76$
*   $y_{\text{min}} \approx -0.759$

$$R = \frac{1.76 - (-0.759)}{2} \approx 1.26$$

gdc.instructions: Use the G-Solv function (or equivalent) to find the 'MAX' and 'MIN' values of the graphed function.

ti84.instructions:
Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC).
Select '4: maximum'. Move the cursor to the left and right of the peak and press [ENTER].
Select '3: minimum'. Move the cursor to the left and right of the valley and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Click on the highest and lowest points of the curve to display the coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the maximum and minimum y-values to determine R.

steps.title: Calculate phase shift φ
steps.content:
The general form is $h(t) = R \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t - \varphi\right) + C$. 

A standard sine wave has its first maximum when its argument is $\frac{\pi}{2}$. Using our GDC, we find the $t$-coordinate of the first peak is $t_{\text{max}} \approx 5.903$. We set up the equation:

$$\frac{\pi}{6}(5.903) - \varphi = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

Rearranging for $\varphi$:

$$\varphi = \frac{\pi}{6}(5.903) - \frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.52$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate (π/6)*5.903 - (π/2) on the main screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of φ using the x-coordinate of the maximum.

steps.title: State final values
steps.content:
Based on our graphical analysis, the function is successfully written in the required form. Correct to 3 significant figures, we have:

*   $R = 1.26$
*   $\varphi = 1.52$
*   $C = 0.500$

$$h(t) = 1.26\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t - 1.52\right) + 0.500$$

questions.specification: Consider the trigonometric functions $\sin x$ and $\sin 2x$ forming the first 2 terms of a geometric sequence where $\sin x \neq 0$. It is given that the condition $0 < |\cos x| < \frac{1}{2}$ is satisfied.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Number and Algebra > SL 1.8—Sum of infinite geo sequence > Question Type 1: Finding the sum of infinite convergent geometric sequences
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Finding the sum of infinite convergent geometric sequences
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) require using the convergence condition and formula for the sum to infinity of a geometric series.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Number and Algebra > SL 1.3—Geometric sequences and series > Question Type 2: Finding the common ratio given two terms
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Finding the common ratio given two terms
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) finds the common ratio r from the first two terms of a geometric sequence: r = (sin 2x)/(sin x), using trig identities and the given cosine constraint.

parts.content: Hence find the common ratio $r$.
parts.markscheme:
- Find the common ratio by dividing consecutive terms: $r = \frac{\sin 2x}{\sin x}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute the double angle formula $\sin 2x = 2\sin x \cos x$ to obtain $r = \frac{2\sin x \cos x}{\sin x} = 2\cos x$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: State common ratio formula
steps.content:
For any geometric sequence, the common ratio $r$ is found by dividing a term by the previous term. Using the first two terms $u_1$ and $u_2$:

$$r = \frac{u_2}{u_1}$$

steps.title: Substitute sequence terms
steps.content:
The question gives the **first 2 terms** as $\sin x$ and $\sin 2x$. Substitute these into the formula, with $u_1 = \sin x$ and $u_2 = \sin 2x$:

$$r = \frac{\sin 2x}{\sin x}$$

steps.title: Apply double angle identity
steps.content:
To simplify the numerator, we use the double angle identity for sine from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet:

$$\sin 2x = 2\sin x \cos x$$

Substituting this into our expression for $r$:

$$r = \frac{2\sin x \cos x}{\sin x}$$

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
Since we are given that $\sin x \neq 0$, we can divide both the numerator and denominator by $\sin x$ to find the simplest form:

$$r = 2\cos x$$

parts.content: Determine the sum to infinity.
parts.markscheme:
- Verify the convergence condition $|r| < 1$: $|2\cos x| = 2|\cos x| < 2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = 1$, hence the sum to infinity exists <marks>R1</marks>

- Find the sum to infinity: $S_\infty = \frac{a}{1-r} = \frac{\sin x}{1-2\cos x}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award full marks only if convergence condition is correctly stated and final expression is correct</note>

steps.title: Find the common ratio
steps.content:
To find the sum of a geometric sequence, we first need the common ratio $r$. We divide the second term by the first term:

$$r = \frac{u_2}{u_1} = \frac{\sin 2x}{\sin x}$$

Using the double angle identity for sine from the data booklet:

$$r = \frac{2\sin x \cos x}{\sin x}$$

Since $\sin x \neq 0$, we can simplify this to:

$$r = 2\cos x$$

steps.title: Verify convergence condition
steps.content:
For the sum to infinity to exist, the sequence must satisfy the convergence condition $|r| < 1$.

Substituting our ratio $r = 2\cos x$:

$$|2\cos x| < 1$$

$$2|\cos x| < 1$$

$$|\cos x| < \frac{1}{2}$$

The question states that $0 < |\cos x| < \frac{1}{2}$, which satisfies this requirement. Therefore, the sum to infinity exists.

steps.title: Apply sum to infinity formula
steps.content:
Now we calculate the sum using the formula $S_\infty = \frac{u_1}{1-r}$.

Substituting $u_1 = \sin x$ and $r = 2\cos x$:

$$S_\infty = \frac{\sin x}{1 - 2\cos x}$$

parts.content: Given that the sum to infinity is $\frac{1}{2}$, find the value of $x\in[0,2\pi]$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to solve the equation using technology: $\frac{\sin x}{1-2\cos x} = \frac{1}{2}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State the values of $x$: $x \approx 1.99$ and $x \approx 5.86$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Reject the invalid solution $x \approx 5.86$ because $|\cos(5.86)| > \frac{1}{2}$ and state the final answer $x \approx 1.99$ <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Set up the sum to infinity equation
steps.content:
The formula for the sum to infinity of a geometric sequence is $$S_{\infty}=\frac{u_{1}}{1-r}$$. 

From the problem, we have:
- $u_1 = \sin x$
- $r = 2\cos x$ (derived from earlier parts or $u_2/u_1$)
- $S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{2}$

Substitute these into the formula:
$$\frac{\sin x}{1-2\cos x} = \frac{1}{2}$$

steps.title: Solve the equation using technology
steps.content: Use your graphing calculator to find the values of $x$ that satisfy this equation in the domain $0 \le x \le 2\pi$. Graph the left side and right side as separate functions and find their points of intersection.

gdc.instructions:
1. In Graph mode, enter y1 = sin(x) / (1 - 2cos(x)) and y2 = 0.5
2. Set View Window x-min: 0, x-max: 6.28
3. Use G-Solve > ISCT (Intersection) to find the x-values

ti84.instructions:

2. Enter Y1 = sin(X) / (1 - 2cos(X))
3. Enter Y2 = 0.5
4. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=2π (approx 6.28)
5. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) > 5:intersect
6. Move cursor to each intersection and press [ENTER] three times

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = \sin(x) / (1 - 2\cos(x))
2. Type y = 0.5
3. Click on the intersection points between x = 0 and x = 2π

calculator.description: Find the intersection points of the two functions

steps.title: Identify potential solutions
steps.content:
The calculator identifies two potential solutions within the domain:

$$x \approx 1.99$$
$$x \approx 5.86$$

steps.title: Check the validity condition
steps.content:
For the sum to infinity to exist, the sequence must converge. The question specifies the condition $$0 < |\cos x| < \frac{1}{2}$$. We must check this condition for both potential values.

For $x \approx 1.99$:
$$|\cos(1.99)| \approx |-0.41| = 0.41$$
Since $0.41 < 0.5$, this solution is **valid**.

For $x \approx 5.86$:
$$|\cos(5.86)| \approx |0.91| = 0.91$$
Since $0.91 > 0.5$, this solution is **invalid**.

gdc.instructions:
1. In Run-Matrix mode
2. Calculate Abs(cos(1.99))
3. Calculate Abs(cos(5.86))

1. Return to home screen [2nd] [MODE] (QUIT)
2. Calculate abs(cos(1.99))
3. Calculate abs(cos(5.86))

desmos.instructions: Type abs(cos(1.99)) and abs(cos(5.86)) to check values

calculator.description: Verify the condition |cos(x)| < 0.5

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Reject the solution $x \approx 5.86$. The only valid value for $x$ is:

$$x \approx 1.99$$

steps.title: Find the common ratio
steps.content:
First, we determine the common ratio $r$ of the geometric sequence. The first term is $u_1 = \sin x$ and the second term is $u_2 = \sin 2x$.

Using the double angle identity $$\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$ from the data booklet:

$$r = \frac{u_2}{u_1} = \frac{\sin 2x}{\sin x} = \frac{2\sin x \cos x}{\sin x} = 2\cos x$$

(Note: We can divide by $\sin x$ because the question states $\sin x \neq 0$)

steps.title: Set up sum equation
steps.content:
The sum to infinity is given as $S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{2}$. Using the sum to infinity formula:

$$S_{\infty} = \frac{u_1}{1-r}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\frac{1}{2} = \frac{\sin x}{1 - 2\cos x}$$

Rearranging to isolate the sine term:

$$1 - 2\cos x = 2\sin x$$

steps.title: Form quadratic in cosine
steps.content:
To solve for $x$, we square both sides to convert $\sin^2 x$ into terms of $\cos x$ using the identity $$\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$$:

$$(1 - 2\cos x)^2 = (2\sin x)^2$$

$$1 - 4\cos x + 4\cos^2 x = 4\sin^2 x$$

Substituting $\sin^2 x = 1 - \cos^2 x$:

$$1 - 4\cos x + 4\cos^2 x = 4(1 - \cos^2 x)$$

$$1 - 4\cos x + 4\cos^2 x = 4 - 4\cos^2 x$$

Rearranging into the standard quadratic form $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$:

$$8\cos^2 x - 4\cos x - 3 = 0$$

steps.title: Solve the quadratic equation
steps.content:
We solve for $\cos x$ using the quadratic formula with $a=8$, $b=-4$, and $c=-3$:

$$\cos x = \frac{-(-4) \pm \sqrt{(-4)^2 - 4(8)(-3)}}{2(8)}$$

$$\cos x = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 + 96}}{16}$$

$$\cos x = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{112}}{16}$$

Simplifying the surd $\sqrt{112} = \sqrt{16 \times 7} = 4\sqrt{7}$:

$$\cos x = \frac{4 \pm 4\sqrt{7}}{16} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{7}}{4}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the two roots:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the two roots:

desmos.instructions: Calculate the two roots:

calculator.description: Calculate the two possible values for cos(x)

steps.title: Apply constraints
steps.content:
We evaluate the two possible values:

1.  $\frac{1 + \sqrt{7}}{4} \approx 0.911$
2.  $\frac{1 - \sqrt{7}}{4} \approx -0.411$

The question specifies the condition $0 < |\cos x| < \frac{1}{2}$.

- Since $|0.911| > 0.5$, we **reject** the first solution.
- Since $|-0.411| < 0.5$, we **accept** the second solution.

Therefore, $\cos x = \frac{1 - \sqrt{7}}{4}$.

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
Now we solve for $x$:

$$x = \arccos\left(\frac{1 - \sqrt{7}}{4}\right)$$

Using a calculator:
$$x \approx 1.99 \text{ radians}$$

We must check for extraneous solutions introduced by squaring in Step 2. The original equation was $1 - 2\cos x = 2\sin x$.
- Since $\cos x \approx -0.411$, the term $1 - 2\cos x$ is positive ($1 - 2(-0.411) \approx 1.82$).
- Therefore, $2\sin x$ must be positive, meaning $\sin x > 0$.
- Since $\cos x < 0$ and $\sin x > 0$, the angle must be in Quadrant 2.

The calculator value $1.99$ is in Quadrant 2 ($ \frac{\pi}{2} < 1.99 < \pi $), so it is the correct solution.

gdc.instructions: Press [acos] then enter (1-sqrt(7))/4

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [COS] then enter (1-sqrt(7))/4

desmos.instructions: Type arccos((1-sqrt(7))/4)

calculator.description: Calculate arccos to find angle x

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The value of $x$ is approximately **1.99**.

$x \approx 1.99$

steps.title: Recall sum to infinity formula
steps.content: The question asks us to use the sum to infinity, which is given as $\frac{1}{2}$. We need the formula for the sum of an infinite geometric sequence.

steps.title: Find the common ratio
steps.content:
The first term is $u_1 = \sin x$ and the second term is $u_2 = \sin 2x$. We find the common ratio $r$ by dividing the second term by the first.

Using the double angle identity $$\sin 2\theta=2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$:

$$r = \frac{u_2}{u_1} = \frac{\sin 2x}{\sin x} = \frac{2\sin x \cos x}{\sin x} = 2\cos x$$

steps.title: Formulate the equation
steps.content:
Now we substitute $u_1 = \sin x$, $r = 2\cos x$, and $S_\infty = \frac{1}{2}$ into the sum formula:

$$\frac{\sin x}{1 - 2\cos x} = \frac{1}{2}$$

steps.title: Simplify to linear form
steps.content:
Cross-multiply to remove the fractions and rearrange the terms to form a linear trigonometric equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 2\sin x &= 1(1 - 2\cos x) \\ 2\sin x &= 1 - 2\cos x \\ 2\sin x + 2\cos x &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Convert to harmonic form
steps.content:
We convert the expression $2\sin x + 2\cos x$ into the harmonic form $R\sin(x + \alpha)$.

First, find $R$:
$$R = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2} = \sqrt{2^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2}$$

Next, find $\alpha$:
$$\tan \alpha = \frac{b}{a} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \Rightarrow \alpha = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

Substituting these back, our equation becomes:
$$2\sqrt{2}\sin\left(x + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = 1$$

steps.title: Isolate the sine term
steps.content:
Divide by $2\sqrt{2}$ to isolate the sine function:

$$\sin\left(x + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}$$ 

Let $\theta = x + \frac{\pi}{4}$. We need to solve $\sin \theta = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}$.

steps.title: Find valid angles
steps.content:
First, find the reference angle (principal value):
$$\arcsin\left(\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}\right) \approx 0.361$$

However, we must consider the domain. Since $x \in [0, 2\pi]$, the argument $\theta = x + \frac{\pi}{4}$ must be in the range $\left[\frac{\pi}{4}, 2\pi + \frac{\pi}{4}\right] \approx [0.79, 7.07]$.

The principal value $0.361$ is less than $0.79$, so it is **outside the valid range**. We must look for the next solutions in the sine cycle:

1.  **Quadrant 2:** $\theta_1 = \pi - 0.361 \approx 2.78$
2.  **Next Period (Quadrant 1):** $\theta_2 = 2\pi + 0.361 \approx 6.64$

gdc.instructions:
1. Set angle mode to Radians
2. Compute arcsin(1/(2*sqrt(2)))

**For $x \approx 1.99$:**

Since $0.425 < 0.5$, this solution is **valid**.

**For $x \approx 5.86$:**
$$|\cos(5.86)| \approx |0.906| = 0.906$$
Since $0.906 > 0.5$, this solution is **invalid** and must be rejected.

parts.content: Show that $f(x) = \sin^2(2x)(2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x)$.
parts.markscheme:
- **Use** double angle identity: $\sin 2x = 2\sin x \cos x$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **Apply** double angle identity again: $\sin 4x = 2\sin 2x \cos 2x = 4\sin x \cos x \cos 2x$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **Substitute** and multiply: $\sin x (2\sin x \cos x)(4\sin x \cos x \cos 2x) = 8\sin^3 x \cos^2 x \cos 2x$ <marks>A1</marks>

- **Use** identity $\cos 2x = 1 - 2\sin^2 x$ to get $8\sin^3 x \cos^2 x - 16\sin^5 x \cos^2 x$ <marks>A1A1</marks>

- **Factor** out $4\sin^2 x \cos^2 x$ to obtain $4\sin^2 x \cos^2 x (2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **Substitute** $\sin^2(2x) = (2\sin x \cos x)^2 = 4\sin^2 x \cos^2 x$ <marks>A1</marks>

- **Conclude** $f(x) = \sin^2(2x)(2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x)$ <marks>A1</marks>

8 marks total

<note>Award marks for each correct step in the algebraic manipulation and proper use of trigonometric identities</note>

steps.title: Analyze the function and target
steps.content:
We start with the given function:

$$f(x) = \sin x \sin 2x \sin 4x$$

Our goal is to show that it equals:

$$f(x) = \sin^2(2x)(2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x)$$

Notice the target expression contains a $\sin^2(2x)$ term. Since our original function already has one $\sin 2x$, expanding $\sin 4x$ using a double angle identity is a good strategy to generate the second $\sin 2x$.

steps.title: Expand sin(4x)
steps.content:
We use the double angle identity for sine from the data booklet to expand $\sin 4x$. By treating $4x$ as $2(2x)$, we can write:

$$\sin 4x = 2\sin 2x \cos 2x$$

Substitute this back into $f(x)$:

$$f(x) = \sin x \sin 2x (2\sin 2x \cos 2x)$$

steps.title: Isolate sin²(2x)
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the terms to group the $\sin 2x$ factors together. This creates the $\sin^2(2x)$ term required by the target expression:

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= 2\sin x (\sin 2x \cdot \sin 2x) \cos 2x \\ f(x) &= \sin^2(2x) (2\sin x \cos 2x) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Convert cos(2x) to sine
steps.content:
The remaining part of the target expression, $(2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x)$, involves only sine terms. We need to convert the $\cos 2x$ in our current expression into terms of $\sin x$.

Using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x$ with the double angle identity for cosine:

$$\cos 2x = \cos^2 x - \sin^2 x = (1 - \sin^2 x) - \sin^2 x = 1 - 2\sin^2 x$$

steps.title: Substitute and expand
steps.content:
Substitute $(1 - 2\sin^2 x)$ in place of $\cos 2x$:

$$f(x) = \sin^2(2x) \cdot [2\sin x (1 - 2\sin^2 x)]$$

Finally, expand the term in the brackets by multiplying $2\sin x$ by each term inside:

$$\begin{aligned} 2\sin x(1) &= 2\sin x \\ 2\sin x(-2\sin^2 x) &= -4\sin^3 x \end{aligned}$$

Putting it all together:

$$f(x) = \sin^2(2x)(2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x)$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2 * -2

ti84.instructions: Calculate 2 * -2

desmos.instructions: Calculate 2 * -2

calculator.description: Verify the coefficient multiplication

steps.title: Strategy: Work Backwards
steps.content:
Since the question asks us to show the equality, we can start with the Right-Hand Side (RHS) and simplify it until it matches the Left-Hand Side (LHS). 

We start with the given RHS:
$$RHS = \sin^2(2x)(2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x)$$

steps.title: Factorize the second term
steps.content:
Let's focus on the term in the brackets: $(2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x)$. We can factor out the common term $2\sin x$:

$$2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x = 2\sin x(1 - 2\sin^2 x)$$

steps.title: Identify cosine double angle
steps.content:
The expression $(1 - 2\sin^2 x)$ is a standard form of the double angle identity for cosine. 

Using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x = 1$, we can rewrite $1 - 2\sin^2 x$ as:
$$(1 - \sin^2 x) - \sin^2 x = \cos^2 x - \sin^2 x$$ 

This matches the formula in the data booklet:
$$\cos 2x = \cos^2 x - \sin^2 x$$

So, we can substitute $\cos 2x$ into our expression.

steps.title: Substitute and rearrange
steps.content:
Now we substitute $2\sin x \cos 2x$ back into our main RHS equation:

$$\begin{aligned} RHS &= \sin^2(2x) \cdot [2\sin x \cos 2x] \\ &= \sin^2(2x) \cdot 2 \sin x \cos 2x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Group terms for sine identity
steps.content:
We need to get to $\sin 4x$. We know that $\sin 4x = 2\sin 2x \cos 2x$. 

Let's expand $\sin^2(2x)$ into $\sin 2x \cdot \sin 2x$ and rearrange the terms to reveal this pattern:

$$\begin{aligned} RHS &= (\sin 2x \cdot \sin 2x) \cdot 2 \sin x \cos 2x \\ &= \sin x \cdot \sin 2x \cdot (2 \sin 2x \cos 2x) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply sine double angle
steps.content:
We can now apply the double angle identity for sine, using $\theta = 2x$:

$$\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Therefore, $(2 \sin 2x \cos 2x)$ becomes $\sin(2 \cdot 2x) = \sin 4x$.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Substituting this back gives us the final expression:

$$RHS = \sin x \sin 2x \sin 4x$$

This is exactly the function $f(x)$ on the LHS. We have successfully shown that the expressions are equal.

steps.title: Define the strategy
steps.content: To prove the identity using the **Comparison of Expanded Forms** method, we will expand both the Left Hand Side (LHS) and the Right Hand Side (RHS) completely into basic terms of $\sin x$ and $\cos x$. Our goal is to show that both sides simplify to the exact same algebraic expression.

steps.title: LHS: Apply double angle identity
steps.content:
Starting with the LHS: $$f(x) = \sin x \sin 2x \sin 4x$$

First, we use the double angle identity for $\sin 2x$.

$$\sin 2x = 2\sin x \cos x$$

steps.title: LHS: Expand sin(4x)
steps.content:
Next, we treat $4x$ as $2(2x)$ and apply the double angle identity again for $\sin 4x$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin 4x &= 2\sin 2x \cos 2x \\ &= 2(2\sin x \cos x)\cos 2x \\ &= 4\sin x \cos x \cos 2x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: LHS: Combine and simplify
steps.content:
Substitute these expanded forms back into the original function:

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= \sin x \cdot (2\sin x \cos x) \cdot (4\sin x \cos x \cos 2x) \\ &= 8\sin^3 x \cos^2 x \cos 2x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: LHS: Expand cos(2x) and finalize
steps.content:
We need to expand $\cos 2x$ into terms of $x$. We use the identity $\cos 2x = 1 - 2\sin^2 x$ (derived from $\cos^2 x - \sin^2 x$ by replacing $\cos^2 x$ with $1-\sin^2 x$).

Substituting this into our expression:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{LHS} &= 8\sin^3 x \cos^2 x (1 - 2\sin^2 x) \\ &= 8\sin^3 x \cos^2 x - 16\sin^5 x \cos^2 x \end{aligned}$$

This is the fully expanded form of the LHS.

steps.title: RHS: Expand sin²(2x)
steps.content:
Now we look at the RHS: $$\sin^2(2x)(2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x)$$

First, expand $\sin^2(2x)$ using the double angle identity:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin^2(2x) &= (\sin 2x)^2 \\ &= (2\sin x \cos x)^2 \\ &= 4\sin^2 x \cos^2 x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: RHS: Multiply and finalize
steps.content:
Substitute this back into the RHS expression and distribute:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= 4\sin^2 x \cos^2 x (2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x) \\ &= 4\sin^2 x \cos^2 x (2\sin x) - 4\sin^2 x \cos^2 x (4\sin^3 x) \\ &= 8\sin^3 x \cos^2 x - 16\sin^5 x \cos^2 x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Comparing the two fully expanded forms:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{LHS} &= 8\sin^3 x \cos^2 x - 16\sin^5 x \cos^2 x \\ \text{RHS} &= 8\sin^3 x \cos^2 x - 16\sin^5 x \cos^2 x \end{aligned}$$

Since LHS = RHS, we have shown that:

$$f(x) = \sin^2(2x)(2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x)$$

parts.content: Hence, or otherwise, given that $\sin x \neq 0$ and $\sin 2x \neq 0$, solve the equation $f(x)=0$ for $x \in \left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$.
parts.markscheme:
**Method 1**
- **State** $f(x) = \sin x \sin 2x \sin 4x = 0 \implies \sin 4x = 0$ since $\sin 2x \neq 0$ and $\sin x \neq 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **Solve** $\sin 4x = 0$ when $4x = n\pi$ for $n \in \mathbb{Z}$, hence when $x = \frac{n\pi}{4}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- **Identify** invalid solutions: at $x=0, \sin x=0$ and at $x=\frac{\pi}{2}, \sin 2x=0$ hence those are not possible <marks>R1</marks>

- **State** the only valid answer is $\frac{\pi}{4}$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Method 2**
- **State** $f(x) = \sin^2(2x)(2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x) = 0$ means $2\sin x - 4\sin^3 x = 0$ since $\sin 2x \neq 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **Simplify** to $2\sin x = 4\sin^3 x \implies 2 = 4\sin^2 x$ since $\sin x \neq 0$ <marks>A1</marks>

- **Solve** for $\sin x$: $\sin^2 x = \frac{1}{2} \implies \sin x = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ due to our interval <marks>R1</marks>

- **Calculate** $x = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Apply Zero Product Property
steps.content:
To solve $f(x) = 0$, we start by setting the given function equal to zero:

$$f(x) = \sin x \sin 2x \sin 4x = 0$$

The **Zero Product Property** states that if the product of factors is zero, at least one of the factors must be zero. This gives us three possibilities:
$$\sin x = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \sin 2x = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \sin 4x = 0$$

steps.title: Eliminate non-zero factors
steps.content:
The question specifies two constraints: $\sin x \neq 0$ and $\sin 2x \neq 0$. These constraints eliminate the first two possibilities.

Therefore, we only need to solve the remaining equation:
$$\sin 4x = 0$$

steps.title: Find domain for 4x
steps.content:
We need to find solutions for $x$ in the interval $\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$. Since our equation is in terms of $4x$, we must adjust the domain for the argument $4x$:

$$0 \le x \le \frac{\pi}{2}$$

Multiplying the inequality by 4:
$$0 \le 4x \le 2\pi$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
We solve $\sin \theta = 0$ for $\theta = 4x$ in the domain $[0, 2\pi]$. The sine function is zero at integer multiples of $\pi$.

$$4x = 0, \; \pi, \; 2\pi$$

Dividing by 4 to find $x$:
$$x = 0, \; \frac{\pi}{4}, \; \frac{\pi}{2}$$

1. Go to Graph mode
2. Enter y = sin(4x)
3. Set View Window x from 0 to pi/2
4. Find Roots/Zeroes

1. Press [Y=]
2. Enter Y1 = sin(4X)
3. Press [WINDOW]
4. Set Xmin = 0, Xmax = pi/2
5. Press [GRAPH]
6. Observe where the graph crosses the x-axis (y=0)

calculator.description: Visualize the solutions by graphing y = sin(4x)

steps.title: Verify solutions against constraints
steps.content:
Now we must check which of these solutions are valid given the original constraints.

1. **Check $x=0$:**
   $\sin x = \sin 0 = 0$. This violates $\sin x \neq 0$.

2. **Check $x=\frac{\pi}{2}$:**
   $\sin 2x = \sin\left(2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}\right) = \sin \pi = 0$. This violates $\sin 2x \neq 0$.

3. **Check $x=\frac{\pi}{4}$:**
   $\sin x = \sin \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \neq 0$
   $\sin 2x = \sin \frac{\pi}{2} = 1 \neq 0$
   This solution is valid.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The only valid solution in the given interval is:

$$x = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
We need to solve for $x$ where $f(x) = 0$. Using the definition of the function given in the specification:

$$f(x) = \sin x \sin 2x \sin 4x = 0$$

steps.title: Apply double angle identity
steps.content:
Following the **Double Angle Expansion** approach, we apply the double angle identity $\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$ to the $\sin 4x$ term. Here, we treat $4x$ as $2(2x)$.

$$\sin 4x = 2\sin 2x \cos 2x$$

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
Now we substitute this expansion back into our original equation:

$$\sin x \sin 2x (2\sin 2x \cos 2x) = 0$$

Multiplying the terms together gives:

$$2\sin x \sin^2 2x \cos 2x = 0$$

steps.title: Cancel non-zero terms
steps.content:
The question specifies that $\sin x \neq 0$ and $\sin 2x \neq 0$. This implies that $\sin^2 2x$ is also non-zero. 

We can divide both sides of the equation by the non-zero factor $2\sin x \sin^2 2x$ to isolate the remaining term:

$$\cos 2x = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
We need to find $x$ in the interval $[0, \frac{\pi}{2}]$. 

For the domain $0 \le x \le \frac{\pi}{2}$, the argument $2x$ ranges from $0$ to $\pi$ ($0 \le 2x \le \pi$).

The cosine function is zero at $\frac{\pi}{2}$ within this range.

$$\begin{aligned} 2x &= \frac{\pi}{2} \\ x &= \frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Verify solution
steps.content:
We should verify that $x = \frac{\pi}{4}$ satisfies the original constraints. 

At $x = \frac{\pi}{4}$:
- $\sin(\frac{\pi}{4}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \neq 0$
- $\sin(2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}) = \sin(\frac{\pi}{2}) = 1 \neq 0$

Both conditions are met, so the solution is valid.

parts.content:
Show that the equation may be written as
\[
2\cos^2x-\sin x-2=0.
\]
parts.markscheme:
- Use $\cos(2x)=2\cos^2x-1$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute into the original equation: $(2\cos^2x-1)-\sin x=1$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Simplify: $2\cos^2x-1-\sin x-1=0 \Rightarrow 2\cos^2x-\sin x-2=0$. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the given equation
steps.content:
We start with the equation given in the question:

$$\cos(2x)-\sin x=1$$

Our goal is to show that this can be written as $$2\cos^2x-\sin x-2=0$$. Notice that the target equation contains a $\cos^2x$ term instead of $\cos(2x)$, which suggests we need to use a double angle identity.

steps.title: Select the double angle identity
steps.content:
We use the double angle identity for cosine. The data booklet provides the formula:

$$\cos 2\theta=\cos^{2}\theta-\sin^{2}\theta$$

By replacing $\sin^2\theta$ with $1-\cos^2\theta$, we get the standard variation that involves only cosine:

$$\cos(2x) = 2\cos^2x - 1$$

steps.title: Substitute into the equation
steps.content:
Substitute $\cos(2x) = 2\cos^2x - 1$ into the original equation:

$$(2\cos^2x - 1) - \sin x = 1$$

steps.title: Rearrange to the target form
steps.content:
Subtract 1 from both sides to set the equation to zero:

$$2\cos^2x - 1 - \sin x - 1 = 0$$

Combine the constant terms ($-1 - 1 = -2$) to obtain the final required form:

$$2\cos^2x - \sin x - 2 = 0$$

steps.title: State the initial equation
steps.content:
We begin with the equation given in the question:

$$\cos(2x) - \sin x = 1$$

steps.title: Use double angle identity
steps.content: We need to replace the double angle term $\cos(2x)$. We use the standard double angle identity found in the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet.

steps.title: Substitute into the equation
steps.content:
Substituting $\cos(2x) = \cos^2x - \sin^2x$ into our equation:

$$(\cos^2x - \sin^2x) - \sin x = 1$$

steps.title: Use Pythagorean identity
steps.content:
The required final equation contains $\cos^2x$ but not $\sin^2x$. We can convert $\sin^2x$ using the Pythagorean identity from the data booklet.

Rearranging $$\cos^{2}\theta+\sin^{2}\theta=1$$ gives $$\sin^2x = 1 - \cos^2x$$

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
Substitute $(1 - \cos^2x)$ for $\sin^2x$ in our equation:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos^2x - (1 - \cos^2x) - \sin x &= 1 \\ \cos^2x - 1 + \cos^2x - \sin x &= 1 \\ 2\cos^2x - \sin x - 1 &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Final rearrangement
steps.content:
Subtract 1 from both sides to set the equation to zero, matching the required form:

$$\begin{aligned} 2\cos^2x - \sin x - 1 - 1 &= 0 \\ 2\cos^2x - \sin x - 2 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Strategy: Working backwards
steps.content:
To show the equivalence, we will start with the target equation given in the "Show that" part and manipulate it algebraically to arrive back at the original equation. 

Starting equation:
$$2\cos^2x - \sin x - 2 = 0$$

steps.title: Isolate the double angle term
steps.content:
We need to relate $\cos^2x$ to $\cos(2x)$. A key form of the double angle identity involves $2\cos^2x - 1$. 

Let's split the constant $-2$ into $-1 - 1$ and rearrange the equation to group these terms:

$$\begin{aligned} 2\cos^2x - 1 - 1 - \sin x &= 0 \\ (2\cos^2x - 1) - \sin x &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply double angle identity
steps.content:
Now we identify the expression inside the brackets as a double angle identity. 

Using the formulas $\cos 2\theta=\cos^{2}\theta-\sin^{2}\theta$ and $\cos^{2}\theta+\sin^{2}\theta=1$ from the data booklet, we can derive the form needed here:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos(2x) &= \cos^2x - (1 - \cos^2x) \\ &= 2\cos^2x - 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Recover the original equation
steps.content:
Substitute $\cos(2x)$ for $(2\cos^2x - 1)$ in our rearranged equation:

$$\cos(2x) - \sin x = 1$$

This matches the original equation provided in the question, confirming that the form $2\cos^2x - \sin x - 2 = 0$ is correct.

parts.content: Express \(\cos^2x\) in terms of \(\sin x\), and hence obtain a quadratic equation in \(\sin x\).
parts.markscheme:
- Use identity $\cos^2x=1-\sin^2x$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute: $2(1-\sin^2x)-\sin x-2=0 \Rightarrow 2-2\sin^2x-\sin x-2=0$. 
- Obtain $2\sin^2x+\sin x=0$. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Apply double angle identity
steps.content:
To solve this equation, we first use a version of the double angle identity for $\cos(2x)$. Based on our chosen approach, we substitute $\cos(2x)$ with $2\cos^2 x - 1$.

Substituting this into the original equation:

$$(2\cos^2 x - 1) - \sin x = 1$$

steps.title: Express cosine using sine
steps.content:
Next, we need to express $\cos^2 x$ in terms of $\sin x$. We use the Pythagorean identity from the data booklet:

$$\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x = 1 \implies \cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x$$

Now, substitute this into our equation from Step 0:

$$2(1 - \sin^2 x) - 1 - \sin x = 1$$

steps.title: Simplify to quadratic form
steps.content:
Finally, we simplify the equation to obtain the quadratic form in $\sin x$. 

$$\begin{aligned} 2 - 2\sin^2 x - 1 - \sin x &= 1 \\ 1 - 2\sin^2 x - \sin x &= 1 \\ -2\sin^2 x - \sin x &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Multiplying the entire equation by $-1$ to make the leading coefficient positive, we get:

$$2\sin^2 x + \sin x = 0$$

methods.label: Double Angle Identity for cos²x - sin²x

steps.title: Apply the double angle identity
steps.content:
To start solving $\cos(2x) - \sin x = 1$, we need to get everything in terms of the same angle. We'll use the double angle identity for $\cos 2\theta$ from your data booklet:

$$\cos 2\theta = \cos^{2}\theta - \sin^{2}\theta$$

Substituting this into our equation gives us:

$$(\cos^2 x - \sin^2 x) - \sin x = 1$$

steps.title: Express cos²x using sine
steps.content:
The question asks us to express $\cos^2 x$ in terms of $\sin x$. We use the Pythagorean identity:

$$\cos^{2}\theta + \sin^{2}\theta = 1$$

By rearranging this, we find that:

$$\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x$$

This is a crucial step that earns you the first mark (**M1**) in the IB markscheme.

steps.title: Form the quadratic equation
steps.content:
Now, we substitute $1 - \sin^2 x$ back into our equation from Step 0 and simplify:

$$\begin{aligned} (1 - \sin^2 x - \sin^2 x) - \sin x &= 1 \\ 1 - 2\sin^2 x - \sin x &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

Subtract 1 from both sides to set the equation to zero:

$$-2\sin^2 x - \sin x = 0$$

Multiplying the entire equation by $-1$ gives us the standard quadratic form required for the second mark (**A1**):

$$2\sin^2 x + \sin x = 0$$

parts.content: Solve \(2\sin^2x+\sin x=0\) exactly for \(\sin x\).
parts.markscheme:
- Factor the equation: $\sin x(2\sin x+1)=0$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Hence $\sin x=0$ or $\sin x=-\dfrac{1}{2}$. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the common factor
steps.content:
Hi there! Let's solve this trigonometric equation by using a common algebraic technique: factoring. 

We start with the equation provided in the question:

$$2\sin^2x + \sin x = 0$$

Notice that both terms on the left side share a common factor of $\sin x$. In IB Math, whenever you see a quadratic-style equation like this where one side is zero, looking for a common factor is a great first step!

steps.title: Factorize the expression
steps.content:
Now, we factor out $\sin x$ from the expression. This transforms the sum into a product of two factors, which is much easier to solve.

$$\sin x(2\sin x + 1) = 0$$

Performing this step correctly earns you the first mark (**M1**) in this problem.

steps.title: Solve for sin x
steps.content:
Using the **zero-product property**, if two things multiply to give zero, then at least one of them must be zero. We set each factor to zero individually:

1.  $\sin x = 0$
2.  $2\sin x + 1 = 0$

Solving the second equation for $\sin x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2\sin x &= -1 \\ \sin x &= -\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

So, the final exact values for $\sin x$ are:

$$\sin x = 0, \sin x = -\frac{1}{2}$$

Finding both of these values correctly earns you the final mark (**A1**). This technique is a key part of **Topic 3: Solving trig equations** in your syllabus.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graph menu.
2. Sketch f(x) = 2(sin(x))^2 + sin(x).
3. Use the G-Solv or Root tool to find the zeros of the function.
4. Calculate sin(x) for these values.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = 2(sin(X))^2 + sin(X).
3. Press [GRAPH].
4. Use [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select '2: zero' to find the x-intercepts.
5. To verify sin(x), calculate the sin of those x-values on the home screen.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = 2(sin(x))^2 + sin(x).
2. Look for the x-intercepts between 0 and 2π.
3. Click on the gray dots at the intercepts to see the x-values.
4. Evaluate sin(x) at those points to confirm they result in 0 or -0.5.

calculator.description: You can verify these solutions by graphing the function and checking the y-values (since the question asks for sin(x)).

steps.title: Substitute for simplicity
steps.content:
To make the equation easier to solve, we can treat it as a quadratic by letting $u = \sin x$. 

The equation $2\sin^2x + \sin x = 0$ then becomes:
$$2u^2 + u + 0 = 0$$

steps.title: Identify quadratic coefficients
steps.content:
We compare our equation $2u^2 + u + 0 = 0$ to the standard quadratic form $au^2 + bu + c = 0$. 

The coefficients are:
- $a = 2$
- $b = 1$
- $c = 0$

steps.title: Apply the quadratic formula
steps.content:
We use the **Quadratic formula** from the data booklet to solve for $u$:

$$u = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$$

Substituting our values $a=2, b=1$, and $c=0$:

$$u = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1^2 - 4(2)(0)}}{2(2)}$$

steps.title: Calculate the values
steps.content:
Now we simplify the expression. First, the discriminant (the part inside the square root):
$$1^2 - 4(2)(0) = 1$$

This gives us two solutions for $u$:
$$\begin{aligned} u &= \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1}}{4} = \frac{0}{4} = 0 \\ u &= \frac{-1 - \sqrt{1}}{4} = \frac{-2}{4} = -\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Equation mode, select Polynomial, Degree 2. Enter a=2, b=1, c=0 and solve.

ti84.instructions: Use the quadratic formula or numeric solver. To use the formula: (-1 + √(1^2 - 4*2*0))/(2*2) and (-1 - √(1^2 - 4*2*0))/(2*2).

desmos.instructions: Find the x-intercepts of the function f(x) = 2x^2 + x.

calculator.description: Calculate the two roots of the quadratic equation.

steps.title: State the final solutions
steps.content:
Since $u = \sin x$, we replace it back to get our final values for $\sin x$:

$$\sin x = 0 \text{ or } \sin x = -\frac{1}{2}$$

parts.content: Determine all exact values of \(x\) in the interval \(0\le x<2\pi\) that satisfy the original equation.
parts.markscheme:
- From $\sin x=0$: $x=0,\pi$. <marks>M1</marks>
- From $\sin x=-\dfrac{1}{2}$: $x=\dfrac{7\pi}{6},\dfrac{11\pi}{6}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- State all four solutions: $x=0,\pi,\dfrac{7\pi}{6},\dfrac{11\pi}{6}$. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Select the double-angle identity
steps.content:
To solve the equation, we need to express all terms using the same trigonometric function. We start with the double-angle identity for cosine from the data booklet:

$$\cos 2\theta = \cos^2 \theta - \sin^2 \theta$$

Since the other term in our equation is $\sin x$, we use the identity $\cos^2 \theta + \sin^2 \theta = 1$ to rewrite this as:

$$\cos 2x = (1 - \sin^2 x) - \sin^2 x = 1 - 2\sin^2 x$$

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
Now, substitute $1 - 2\sin^2 x$ for $\cos(2x)$ into the original equation:

$$\begin{aligned} (1 - 2\sin^2 x) - \sin x &= 1 \\ 1 - 2\sin^2 x - \sin x &= 1 \\ -2\sin^2 x - \sin x &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Multiplying by $-1$ to make it easier to factorize, we get:

$$2\sin^2 x + \sin x = 0$$

steps.title: Factorize the equation
steps.content:
We can factorize this quadratic-style equation by taking out the common factor of $\sin x$:

$$\sin x (2\sin x + 1) = 0$$

For the product to be zero, one of the factors must be zero. This gives us two cases:

1.  $\sin x = 0$
2.  $2\sin x + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \sin x = -\frac{1}{2}$

steps.title: Solve for sin x = 0
steps.content:
We look for values of $x$ in the interval $0 \le x < 2\pi$ where the sine is zero. On the unit circle, the sine is the $y$-coordinate. This occurs at the start and the halfway point of the circle:

$$x = 0, \quad x = \pi$$

steps.title: Solve for sin x = -1/2
steps.content:
Next, find values where $\sin x = -\frac{1}{2}$. The reference angle for $\sin x = \frac{1}{2}$ is $\frac{\pi}{6}$. Since sine is negative, we look in the third and fourth quadrants:

- Quadrant III: $x = \pi + \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{7\pi}{6}$
- Quadrant IV: $x = 2\pi - \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{11\pi}{6}$

steps.title: State all exact values
steps.content:
By combining all our results, we get the four exact solutions in the given interval:

$$x = 0, \pi, \frac{7\pi}{6}, \frac{11\pi}{6}$$

steps.title: Define functions for graphing
steps.content:
To solve the equation graphically, we split the equation into two separate functions. We want to find where the left-hand side is equal to the right-hand side. 

Enter these into your GDC:
$$\begin{aligned} y_1 &= \cos(2x) - \sin x \\ y_2 &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In the Graph menu, enter f1(x) = cos(2x)-sin(x) and f2(x) = 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=]. Enter 'cos(2X)-sin(X)' in Y1 and '1' in Y2.

desmos.instructions: Type y = cos(2x)-sin(x) in the first row and y = 1 in the second row.

calculator.description: Enter the equations into the function editor.

steps.title: Set the viewing window
steps.content: The question specifies the interval $0 \le x < 2\pi$. Ensure your calculator is in **Radian mode** and adjust your window settings so you can see all intersections within this range.

gdc.instructions: Press Menu -> Window/Zoom -> Window Settings. Set Xmin to 0 and Xmax to 2*pi.

ti84.instructions: Press [WINDOW]. Set Xmin = 0, Xmax = 2π, Xscl = π/2. Set Ymin = -2, Ymax = 2.

desmos.instructions: Click the wrench icon. Set the x-axis from 0 to 2π.

calculator.description: Adjust the window to match the domain.

steps.title: Find intersection points
steps.content:
Use your GDC's intersection tool to find the $x$-coordinates where the graphs of $y_1$ and $y_2$ cross. In the interval $[0, 2\pi)$, you should see four distinct intersection points.

Your calculator will provide the following decimal values:
- $x_1 = 0$
- $x_2 \approx 3.14159$
- $x_3 \approx 3.66519$
- $x_4 \approx 5.75958$

gdc.instructions: Press Menu -> Analyze Graph -> Intersection. Swipe across each intersection point.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC), then select '5: intersect'. Select the first curve, second curve, and move the cursor near an intersection for the guess.

desmos.instructions: Click on the points where the two lines intersect to reveal their coordinates.

calculator.description: Use the intersection tool to find the coordinates.

steps.title: Identify exact values
steps.content:
The question asks for **exact values**. We can convert our decimal results into multiples of $\pi$ by dividing each decimal by $\pi$ (or by recognizing standard trigonometric angles):

1. $x = 0$ is already exact.
2. $x \approx 3.14159$ is exactly $\pi$.
3. $x \approx 3.66519$: dividing $3.66519 / \pi \approx 1.1666...$, which is $\frac{7}{6}$, so $x = \frac{7\pi}{6}$.
4. $x \approx 5.75958$: dividing $5.75958 / \pi \approx 1.8333...$, which is $\frac{11}{6}$, so $x = \frac{11\pi}{6}$.

These values align with the markscheme roots where $\sin x = 0$ and $\sin x = -\frac{1}{2}$.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The exact solutions in the interval $0 \le x < 2\pi$ are:
$$x = 0, \pi, \frac{7\pi}{6}, \frac{11\pi}{6}$$

parts.content: Sketch the graphs of \(y=\cos(2x)-\sin x\) and \(y=1\) for \(0\le x\le2\pi\), indicating the approximate intersection points corresponding to your solutions from part 4.
parts.markscheme:
- Reasonable sketch of $y=\cos(2x)-\sin x$ showing oscillation with correct general shape and period $2\pi$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Draw horizontal line $y=1$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Show four intersection points in positions consistent with the four solutions found. <marks>A1</marks>

![Graph of y=cos(2x)-sin(x) and y=1](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/84abc89c-6659-42fe-830c-597f05526cd6.png)

3 marks total

steps.title: Set up the graph
steps.content:
To sketch these functions, first draw a coordinate system with $x$ from $0$ to $2\pi$ and $y$ from $-2$ to $2$. The simplest part is drawing the horizontal line:

$$y = 1$$

This line serves as a reference for our intersection points.

steps.title: Evaluate at quadrantal angles
steps.content:
Using the **Point-by-Point Plotting** approach, evaluate $y = \cos(2x) - \sin(x)$ at the main quadrantal angles to find the "skeleton" of the graph:

$$\begin{aligned} x = 0: & \quad \cos(0) - \sin(0) = 1 - 0 = 1 \\ x = \frac{\pi}{2}: & \quad \cos(\pi) - \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = -1 - 1 = -2 \\ x = \pi: & \quad \cos(2\pi) - \sin(\pi) = 1 - 0 = 1 \\ x = \frac{3\pi}{2}: & \quad \cos(3\pi) - \sin\left(\frac{3\pi}{2}\right) = -1 - (-1) = 0 \\ x = 2\pi: & \quad \cos(4\pi) - \sin(2\pi) = 1 - 0 = 1 \end{aligned}$$

Plot these points: $(0, 1)$, $(\frac{\pi}{2}, -2)$, $(\pi, 1)$, $(\frac{3\pi}{2}, 0)$, and $(2\pi, 1)$.

gdc.instructions: In the Graphing menu, enter the function f1(x) = cos(2x) - sin(x). Use the Trace tool or a Table with steps of π/2 to find the y-values.

ti84.instructions: Go to [Y=] and enter Y1 = cos(2X) - sin(X). Go to [2nd] [WINDOW] (TBLSET) and set TblStart = 0 and ΔTbl = π/2. Then press [2nd] [GRAPH] (TABLE) to see the values.

desmos.instructions: Type y = cos(2x) - sin(x) and click on the curve at x = 0, π/2, π, 3π/2, and 2π to see the coordinates.

calculator.description: Evaluate the function at key points to verify the 'skeleton' coordinates.

steps.title: Anchor intersection points
steps.content:
From the previous part of the question, we know the solutions for $\cos(2x) - \sin(x) = 1$ are $x = 0, \pi, \frac{7\pi}{6},$ and $\frac{11\pi}{6}$. Since the domain for this sketch includes $2\pi$, we also include $(2\pi, 1)$.

Mark these intersection points on your $y=1$ line:

$$\left(\pi, 1\right), \quad \left(\frac{7\pi}{6}, 1\right), \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{11\pi}{6}, 1\right)$$ 

(Plus the endpoints at $0$ and $2\pi$ if applicable).

highlights.text: indicating the approximate intersection points corresponding to your solutions from part 4

steps.title: Refine the curve shape
steps.content:
To get the correct general shape, notice how the graph behaves between the intersections:

1. From $x = 0$ to $x = \pi$: The graph dips down to a minimum of $-2$ at $x = \frac{\pi}{2}$ and returns to $1$ at $x = \pi$.
2. From $x = \pi$ to $x = \frac{7\pi}{6}$: The graph actually rises slightly **above** $y=1$ before returning to the intersection at $x = \frac{7\pi}{6}$.
3. From $x = \frac{7\pi}{6}$ to $x = \frac{11\pi}{6}$: The graph dips down to $0$ at $x = \frac{3\pi}{2}$ and returns to $1$ at $x = \frac{11\pi}{6}$.
4. From $x = \frac{11\pi}{6}$ to $x = 2\pi$: The graph again rises slightly **above** $y=1$ before finishing at $(2\pi, 1)$.

These small "bumps" are essential for the curve to pass through all four identified intersection points correctly.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the function at x = 3.3.

ti84.instructions: In the Home screen, ensure mode is in RADIANS. Enter cos(2*3.3) - sin(3.3).

desmos.instructions: Click on the graph between x = π and x = 7π/6 to see the local maximum.

calculator.description: Check a value between π and 7π/6 to confirm the 'bump' above y=1.

steps.title: Finalize the sketch
steps.content:
Draw a smooth, oscillating curve through the points you've plotted. Ensure the graph is periodic with period $2\pi$ and labels the intersection points clearly.

According to the markscheme, you earn:
- **M1** for the correct oscillating shape and $2\pi$ period.
- **A1** for the horizontal line at $y=1$.
- **A1** for showing the four intersection points clearly marked where the curve meets the line.

highlights.text: Reasonable sketch of y=cos(2x)-sin(x) showing oscillation with correct general shape and period 2π

steps.title: Set up your GDC
steps.content:
To sketch these graphs accurately, start by entering the equations into your graphing calculator. Ensure your calculator is in **Radian mode**.

Set your viewing window to capture the full domain $0 \le x \le 2\pi$:
- **Xmin:** $0$
- **Xmax:** $2\pi \approx 6.28$
- **Ymin:** $-2.5$
- **Ymax:** $2$

Enter the functions:
- $Y_1 = \cos(2x) - \sin(x)$
- $Y_2 = 1$

steps.title: Identify key coordinates
steps.content:
Use the 'Trace' or 'Table' feature to find specific coordinates that define the shape of $y = \cos(2x) - \sin(x)$:

- **Start/End Points:** $(0, 1)$ and $(2\pi, 1)$
- **Deep Minimum:** $(\frac{\pi}{2}, -2)$
- **Crossing/Peak:** $(\pi, 1)$
- **Local Minimum:** $(\frac{3\pi}{2}, 0)$

Notice that the graph rises slightly above $y=1$ between $x=\pi$ and $x=\frac{7\pi}{6}$, and again between $x=\frac{11\pi}{6}$ and $x=2\pi$.

gdc.instructions: Use 'Analyze Graph' -> 'Minimum' or 'Maximum' to pinpoint the key turning points of the curve.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [TRACE] and select 'minimum' or 'maximum' to find the coordinates of the peaks and troughs.

calculator.description: Find local extrema and points of interest.

steps.title: Draw the horizontal line
steps.content: On your exam paper, draw a set of axes. Draw a straight horizontal line across the entire domain at the level $y=1$. Label this line clearly as $y=1$.

steps.title: Sketch the curve
steps.content:
Draw the curve $y = \cos(2x) - \sin(x)$ following these movements:
1. Start at $(0, 1)$.
2. Curve downwards to a deep trough at $(\frac{\pi}{2}, -2)$.
3. Curve upwards, passing through $(\pi, 1)$.
4. Rise slightly above $y=1$, then dip back down to cross $y=1$ at $x \approx \frac{7\pi}{6}$.
5. Continue dipping to a local minimum at $(\frac{3\pi}{2}, 0)$.
6. Curve back up to cross $y=1$ at $x \approx \frac{11\pi}{6}$.
7. Rise slightly above $y=1$ before ending at $(2\pi, 1)$.

steps.title: Mark intersection points
steps.content:
Indicate the four intersection points where the curve meets the line $y=1$ within the domain $0 \le x < 2\pi$. These correspond to your solutions from Part 4:
- $x = 0$
- $x = \pi$
- $x = \frac{7\pi}{6} \approx 3.67$
- $x = \frac{11\pi}{6} \approx 5.76$

Ensure these points are clearly marked on your sketch.

highlights.text: indicating the approximate intersection points corresponding to your solutions from part 4

steps.title: Define the component functions
steps.content:
To use the **Addition of Ordinates** method, we treat $y = \cos(2x) - \sin x$ as the sum of two separate functions:

1. $y_1 = \cos(2x)$
2. $y_2 = -\sin x$

By sketching these two graphs on the same set of axes, we can visually add their $y$-values (ordinates) at various $x$-intervals to find points on our final curve.

steps.title: Sketch the component graphs
steps.content:
First, we sketch our two components over the domain $[0, 2\pi]$:

*   **$y_1 = \cos(2x)$**: This is a cosine wave with a period of $\frac{2\pi}{2} = \pi$. It completes two full cycles in the given domain, oscillating between $1$ and $-1$.
*   **$y_2 = -\sin x$**: This is a standard sine wave reflected across the $x$-axis. It has a period of $2\pi$, starting at $(0,0)$, reaching a minimum of $-1$ at $x = \frac{\pi}{2}$, and a maximum of $1$ at $x = \frac{3\pi}{2}$.

steps.title: Calculate key ordinate sums
steps.content:
Now, we add the $y$-values of our components at specific points to locate the peaks, troughs, and intercepts of the composite graph $y = \cos(2x) - \sin x$:

$$\begin{aligned} x=0: & \quad y = 1 + 0 = 1 \\ x=\frac{\pi}{2}: & \quad y = -1 + (-1) = -2 \\ x=\pi: & \quad y = 1 + 0 = 1 \\ x=\frac{3\pi}{2}: & \quad y = -1 + 1 = 0 \\ x=2\pi: & \quad y = 1 + 0 = 1 \end{aligned}$$

Notice that the graph reaches a deep minimum of $-2$ at $x = \frac{\pi}{2}$.

steps.title: Sketch and identify intersections
steps.content:
Finally, we draw the composite curve by connecting our calculated points with a smooth oscillating shape and add the horizontal line $y=1$.

According to the solutions from Part 4, we expect the graph of $y = \cos(2x) - \sin x$ to intersect $y=1$ at four specific points within the domain:
1. $x = 0$
2. $x = \pi$
3. $x = \frac{7\pi}{6} \approx 3.67$
4. $x = \frac{11\pi}{6} \approx 5.76$

Ensure your sketch shows the curve crossing the line $y=1$ at these locations.

2. Set the view window for x from 0 to 2π.
3. Use the 'G-Solv' or 'Intersection' tool to find the four points where the graphs meet.

questions.specification: Consider the trigonometric equation $3\cos(2x) = 3\sin(x) - 1$.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: ​​Rewriting equations to be in form of a quadratic
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The equation 3cos(2x)=3sin(x)−1 is to be rewritten into a quadratic in sin(x) (using cos(2x)=1−2sin^2(x)) and then solved for x over a restricted domain, which matches rewriting trig equations into quadratic form.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.6—Pythagorean identity, double angles > Question Type 3: Rewriting trigonometric equations using the double angle identities and golden rule
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Rewriting trigonometric equations using the double angle identities and golden rule
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) requires use of the double-angle identity for cos(2x) (and related Pythagorean ‘golden rule’ relationships) to convert the equation into a polynomial in sin(x).

parts.content: Express this equation in the form $a\sin^2(x)+b\sin(x)+c=0$, where $a, b, c \in \mathbb{Z}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute the double angle identity: $3\cos(2x)=3(1-2\sin^2(x))=3-6\sin^2(x)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute into the original equation and rearrange: $3-6\sin^2(x)=3\sin(x)-1 \implies 6\sin^2(x)+3\sin(x)-4=0$ <marks>M1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the strategy
steps.content:
Our goal is to write the equation in terms of $\sin(x)$ only. Currently, the equation mixes $\cos(2x)$ and $\sin(x)$. 

To fix this, we need to replace the double angle term $\cos(2x)$ with an expression involving $\sin(x)$. This relates to **Topic 3.6: Pythagorean identity, double angles**.

steps.title: Select the identity
steps.content:
From the data booklet in the **Trigonometry** section, we have the double angle formula for cosine. 

Since the rest of our equation uses $\sin(x)$, we use the version of the identity that depends only on sine: $$\cos(2x) = 1 - 2\sin^2(x)$$ 

*Note: This is derived from $\cos(2\theta) = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta$ and $\cos^2\theta = 1 - \sin^2\theta$.*

steps.title: Substitute into equation
steps.content:
Substitute $1 - 2\sin^2(x)$ for $\cos(2x)$ in the original equation and expand the brackets:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3\cos(2x) &= 3\sin(x) - 1 \\
3(1 - 2\sin^2(x)) &= 3\sin(x) - 1 \\
3 - 6\sin^2(x) &= 3\sin(x) - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

steps.title: Rearrange to quadratic form
steps.content:
To match the required form $a\sin^2(x)+b\sin(x)+c=0$, we move all terms to one side to set the equation to zero. Let's add $6\sin^2(x)$ and subtract $3$ from both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3 - 6\sin^2(x) &= 3\sin(x) - 1 \\
0 &= 6\sin^2(x) + 3\sin(x) - 1 - 3 \\
0 &= 6\sin^2(x) + 3\sin(x) - 4
\end{aligned}
$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate -1 - 3

ti84.instructions: Calculate -1 - 3

desmos.instructions: Calculate -1 - 3

calculator.description: Verify the arithmetic for the constant term

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The equation is now in the required quadratic form where the coefficients $a=6$, $b=3$, and $c=-4$ are all integers.

$$6\sin^2(x) + 3\sin(x) - 4 = 0$$

parts.content: Hence find the value of $x$ given that $-\frac{\pi}{2} \le x \le \frac{\pi}{2}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Solve the quadratic equation for $u = \sin(x)$: $u=\frac{-3\pm\sqrt{9+96}}{12} = \frac{-3\pm\sqrt{105}}{12}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Reject the invalid solution: Since $\sqrt{105}>\sqrt{81}=9$, we have $\frac{-3-\sqrt{105}}{12} < \frac{-3-9}{12} = -1$. As $-1 \le \sin(x) \le 1$, the only valid solution is $\sin(x) = \frac{-3+\sqrt{105}}{12}$ <marks>R1</marks>
- State the final value: $x=\sin^{-1}\left(\frac{-3+\sqrt{105}}{12}\right)$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Substitute double angle identity
steps.content:
To solve the equation for $x$, we first rewrite it using a single trigonometric function. We use the double angle identity for cosine, $\cos(2x) = 1 - 2\sin^2(x)$, to express the equation purely in terms of $\sin(x)$.

Substituting this into the given equation $3\cos(2x) = 3\sin(x) - 1$:

$$3(1 - 2\sin^2(x)) = 3\sin(x) - 1$$

steps.title: Form quadratic equation
steps.content:
Next, we rearrange the terms to form a standard quadratic equation of the form $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$. Let $u = \sin(x)$ for clarity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
3(1 - 2u^2) &= 3u - 1 \\
3 - 6u^2 &= 3u - 1 \\
0 &= 6u^2 + 3u - 4
\end{aligned}
$$

steps.title: Solve for sin(x)
steps.content:
We solve the quadratic equation $6u^2 + 3u - 4 = 0$ using the quadratic formula from the data booklet, where $a=6$, $b=3$, and $c=-4$.

$$\begin{aligned} u &= \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} \\ \sin(x) &= \frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{3^2 - 4(6)(-4)}}{2(6)} \\ \sin(x) &= \frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{9 + 96}}{12} \\ \sin(x) &= \frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{105}}{12} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Menu > Algebra > Polynomial Tools > Find Roots of Polynomial. Enter degree 2. Enter coefficients 6, 3, -4.

ti84.instructions: Press [APPS] > PlySmlt2 > Root Finder. Set order to 2. Enter a2=6, a1=3, a0=-4. Press [GRAPH] (SOLVE).

desmos.instructions: Type the quadratic formula or graph y = 6x^2 + 3x - 4 and find x-intercepts.

calculator.description: Calculate the roots of the quadratic equation 6u² + 3u - 4 = 0

steps.title: Identify valid solution
steps.content:
We now check which value is valid for $\sin(x)$. The range of the sine function is $-1 \le \sin(x) \le 1$.

Using $\sqrt{105} \approx 10.25$, we test the two possible values:
1.  $\frac{-3 + 10.25}{12} \approx 0.60$ (Valid)
2.  $\frac{-3 - 10.25}{12} \approx -1.10$ (Invalid, since $-1.10 < -1$)

Therefore, we reject the negative solution and proceed with:

$$\sin(x) = \frac{-3 + \sqrt{105}}{12}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the approximate values of the roots.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expressions in the home screen to check their decimal values.

desmos.instructions: Type the expressions to see their values.

calculator.description: Verify the values of the two roots

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Finally, we find the value of $x$. The question restricts the domain to $-\frac{\pi}{2} \le x \le \frac{\pi}{2}$. Since our value for $\sin(x)$ is positive, the angle $x$ lies in the first quadrant ($0 \le x \le \frac{\pi}{2}$).

The exact value is:

$$x = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{-3 + \sqrt{105}}{12}\right)$$

questions.specification: Solve the following equations for $x$ in the interval $[0, \pi]$
